content.problemStatement:
There's an N×MN\times MN×M grid. The top-left cell is labelled (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) and the bottom-right is (N,M)(N, M)(N,M).

Each cell is either empty, or has a goose in it.

You want to go from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) to (N,M)(N, M)(N,M), moving only right or down in one step — that is, from (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) you can move to either (x+1,y)(x+1, y)(x+1,y) or (x,y+1)(x, y+1)(x,y+1).

It takes Ax,yA_{x, y}Ax,y​ seconds to step onto cell (x,y)(x, y)(x,y). It is guaranteed that A1,1=0A_{1, 1} = 0A1,1​=0.

Geese, as you know, can be quite aggressive birds.

If you stand on a cell that is adjacent to a goose, it'll attack you; and you need to wait KKK seconds for it to stop.

Multiple geese can attack you on the same cell, and you need KKK seconds for each of them.

You can move out of the cell only after fending off all adjacent geese.

Each goose will attack you at most once; and will disappear after attacking you.

It is allowed to move to a cell that previously contained a goose — of course, as long as you only move one step right or down.

Further, it is guaranteed that there are no geese on the starting and ending cells, as well as any cells adjacent to them.

Find the **minimum** time you need to reach (N,M)(N, M)(N,M) starting from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1).


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains three space-separated integers NNN, MMM, and KKK — the dimensions of the grid and the time loss on being attacked by a goose.
The next NNN lines describe the array AAA. The iii-th of them contains MMM space-separated integers Ai,1,Ai,2,…,Ai,MA_{i, 1}, A_{i, 2}, \ldots, A_{i, M}Ai,1​,Ai,2​,…,Ai,M​.
The next NNN lines describe the positions of the geese. The iii-th of them contains a binary string SiS_iSi​, where 000 represents an empty cell and 111 represents a goose.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the minimum time needed to go from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) to (N,M)(N, M)(N,M).
constraints: SiS_iSi​ is a binary string of length MMM.
Si,j=0S_{i, j} = 0Si,j​=0 for any cell (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) that's a starting cell, destination cell, or one adjacent to them.
The sum of NNN and the sum of MMM across all test cases both won't exceed 100010001000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There's a single goose in the middle of the 3×33\times 33×3 area, and it'll always attack you no matter what path you take.
The optimal path is thus as follows:
(1,1)→(2,1)→(3,1)→(3,2)→(3,3)(1, 1) \to (2, 1) \to (3, 1) \to (3, 2) \to (3, 3)(1,1)→(2,1)→(3,1)→(3,2)→(3,3)
The goose will attack you when you're at (2,1)(2, 1)(2,1).
The total time is 3+3+5+1=123 + 3 + 5 + 1 = 123+3+5+1=12, plus an extra 101010 for the goose attack for 222222 in total.
Test case 222: It's optimal to avoid the goose entirely, and go (1,1)→(1,2)→(1,3)→(2,3)→(3,3)(1, 1) \to (1, 2) \to (1, 3) \to (2, 3) \to (3, 3)(1,1)→(1,2)→(1,3)→(2,3)→(3,3), avoiding the goose entirely.
Test case 333: The optimal path is:
(1,1)→(2,1)→(3,1)→(3,2)→(4,2)→(4,3)(1, 1) \to (2, 1) \to (3, 1) \to (3, 2) \to (4, 2) \to (4, 3)(1,1)→(2,1)→(3,1)→(3,2)→(4,2)→(4,3).
When at (3,1)(3, 1)(3,1), two geese will attack you; each increasing your time by 808080 seconds - it is nevertheless faster than trying to avoid them.
